flashcards.back: A pyramid-shaped organization of different memory types arranged by speed, size, and cost. Fastest (registers) at top, slowest (magnetic tape/cloud) at bottom.
A small, fast memory placed between the CPU and main memory that stores frequently accessed data and instructions to reduce memory access time.
The principle that if a memory location is accessed once, it is likely to be accessed again soon (e.g., loop variables, counters).
The principle that if a memory location is accessed, nearby locations are likely to be accessed soon (e.g., sequential array traversal).
When the requested data is found in the cache memory. The CPU can access it directly without going to main memory.
When the requested data is NOT found in cache and must be fetched from main memory, causing additional delay (miss penalty).
The fraction of memory accesses that result in a cache hit. Calculated as: Hit Ratio = Hits / (Hits + Misses).
Each main memory block maps to exactly one cache line using the formula: Cache Line = (Block Address) mod (Number of Cache Lines). Simple but may cause conflicts.
Any memory block can be placed in any cache line. Most flexible but requires expensive hardware (comparators) to search all lines.
Cache is divided into sets, each containing multiple lines. A block maps to a specific set but can occupy any line within that set (e.g., 4-way set-associative).
Data is written to both cache and main memory simultaneously. Simpler to implement, ensures data consistency, but slower writes.
Data is written only to cache initially. Main memory is updated only when the block is replaced. Faster but requires dirty bit to track modifications.
The additional time required to fetch data from main memory when a cache miss occurs, including the time to access main memory and update the cache.

flashcards.front: What is the role of a tag in cache?
flashcards.back: A tag stores the portion of the main memory address that identifies which memory block is stored in a particular cache line.
The consistency of data stored in local caches of a shared resource. Ensures that reads always return the most recent write value in multiprocessor systems.
